messages.content:
The following text is adapted from Charlotte Brontë''s 1853 novel Villette. Polly is a young child who has come to live with the narrator. Some days elapsed, and it appeared she [Polly] was not likely to take much of a fancy to anybody in the house. She was not exactly naughty or wilful: she was far from disobedient; but an object less conducive to comfort-to tranquillity even-than she presented, it was scarcely possible to have before one's eyes. Which choice best states the main idea of the text?
1
2
(A) Polly preferred to keep to herself.
(B) Polly made the other members of the household uncomfortable.
(C) Polly was mischievous and disruptive.
3
(D) Polly took several days to become comfortable in her new home.
4

The choice that best states the main idea of the text is:

(B) Polly made the other members of the household uncomfortable.

The following text is adapted from Jack London's 1903 novel The Call of the Wild.
And over this great demesne Buck ruled. Here he was born, and here he had lived the four years of his life. It was true, there were other dogs, there could not but be other dogs on so vast a place, but they did not count. They came and went, resided in the populous kennels, or lived obscurely in the recesses of the house after the fashion of Toots, the Japanese pug, or Ysabel, the Mexican hairless,-strange creatures that rarely put nose out of doors or set foot to ground. According to the text, what is true about Buck?
while the house dogs were in charge
indoors.
(A) Buck ruled the outside of the "place," (B) Buck was the most important of all
the dogs on the "place."
(C) Buck is only one of the many different house dogs on the "place."
(D) Buck shares many characteristics with the other dogs on the "place."
(B) Buck was the most important of all the dogs on the "place."
explanation

The excerpt from Jack London's "The Call of the Wild" describes Buck's dominion over a large estate, referred to as a "great demesne," over which he ruled. Buck, having lived there all four years of his life, is established as the foremost dog in this setting. The text acknowledges the presence of other dogs, indicating that it's natural for other dogs to exist in such a vast area, but it distinguishes Buck by noting these other dogs "did not count." They are described as either temporary residents in the kennels or as obscure inhabitants within the house, like Toots, the Japanese pug, or Ysabel, the Mexican hairless, who lead very different lives compared to Buck. These dogs are portrayed as oddities that barely venture outdoors, in stark contrast to Buck's prominence and active life outside.

Given this information:
- Choice (A) inaccurately narrows Buck's dominion to merely the outdoors, without acknowledging his overall significance.
- Choice (B) correctly captures Buck’s established prominence and role as the most important dog in the estate, aligning with the text's emphasis on his ruling status and the insignificance of other dogs in comparison.
- Choice (C) misinterprets Buck's position, suggesting he's just another among many house dogs, which contradicts the text's portrayal of him as uniquely dominant.
- Choice (D) inaccurately suggests Buck is similar to the other dogs, whereas the text clearly sets him apart in his importance and active outdoor life.

Therefore, choice (B) is the most accurate in summarizing Buck's significant and unique status as described in the passage.

The following text is adapted from Oscar Wilde's 1906 humorous short story "The Canterville Ghost." Hiram B. Otis, an American diplomat assigned to England, is purchasing Canterville Chase, an English estate. When Mr. Hiram B. Otis, the American Minister, bought Canterville Chase, everyone told him he was doing a very foolish thing, as there was no doubt at all that the place was haunted. Indeed, Lord Canterville himself, who was a man of the most punctilious honor, had felt it his duty to mention the fact to Mr. Otis when they came to discuss terms... "My Lord," answered the Minister, "I will take the furniture and the ghost at a valuation. I have come from a modern country, where we have everything that money can buy; and with all our spry young fellows painting the Old World red, and carrying off your best actors and prima donnas, I reckon that if there were such a thing as a ghost in Europe, we'd have it at home in a very short time in one of our public museums, or on the road as a show." Based on the text, how does Hiram B. Otis respond to Lord Canterville?
(A) He insists that, because Canterville
Chase is haunted, the price is too high.
(B) He claims that ghosts are not to be feared, as they are on display in American museums.
(C) He states he will hire young men who will carry off the ghost to America.
(D) He asserts he does not believe in
ghosts.

Based on the text, Hiram B. Otis's response to Lord Canterville more closely aligns with:

(D) He asserts he does not believe in ghosts.

Explanation: Hiram B. Otis's response to the mention of a ghost haunting Canterville Chase is characterized by an air of skepticism and light-hearted dismissal typical of someone who does not take the notion of ghosts seriously. His remarks about having everything money can buy in his country and jesting that if ghosts were real, Americans would already have one in a museum or touring as a show, imply a disbelief in the existence of ghosts. While he does not explicitly say "I do not believe in ghosts," the nature of his comments and the tone suggest a skeptical attitude towards the supernatural, rather than fear or concern.

Anthropologists define cooking as the intentional use of heat in a controlled way to process food. Cooking makes the nutrients contained in food more accessible, and
is considered a major development in human evolution because cooked food may have fueled the development of the large, energy-consuming human brain. However, the date when humans began cooking food remains unclear. The previous best estimate of when humans began cooking food was 170,000 years ago, but a recent discovery by Irit Zohar and her team has moved that date to about 600,000 years earlier. Zohar discovered an ancient fish skeleton with teeth that had been heated to a temperature hot enough to cook the fish, but below the temperatures within a fire, indicating that the fish had been placed above or adjacent to a fire. According to the text, why did the teeth of the fish skeleton provide evidence of the earliest known example of cooking by humans?
(A) They indicated the fish had been consumed by humans.
(B) They determined the location of the fish relative to the fire.
(C) They indicated humans were using cooked foods in the evolution of larger brains.
(D) They determined the species and source of the fish.

(B) They determined the location of the fish relative to the fire.

Explanation: The paragraph describes the discovery of an ancient fish skeleton with teeth that had been heated to a specific temperature—hot enough to cook the fish but below the temperatures typically found inside a fire. This detail provides evidence that the fish had been placed in a controlled manner relative to a fire (either above it or adjacent to it) to cook it, rather than thrown directly into the flames. This discovery indicates an intentional use of heat to process food, hence why the position of the fish relative to the fire (inferred from the condition of the teeth) is significant.

A number of natural chemicals extracted from marine organisms such as sponges, algae, and bacteria may provide alternative treatments for cancer. A team of researchers led by Julia Kubanek isolated a number of these chemicals, called bromophycolides, from a type of red algae, Callophycus serratus, and found them to be effective against the cells of several different cancers. These chemicals share a structure that, so far, has only been identified in Callophycus serratus. Therefore, this study suggests that to continue the search for alternative treatments for cancer, it may be helpful to Which choice most logically completes the text?
(A) investigate alternative methods for extracting bromophycolides from Callophycus serratus.
(B) verify the precise effect of Callophycus serratus on healthy cells.
(C) identify how the unique structure of bromophycolides interacts with cancer cells.
(D) determine how to produce bromophycolides from sponges and other marine organisms.
(A) investigate alternative methods for extracting bromophycolides from Callophycus serratus.

The Human Genome Project, completed in 2003, cataloged over 90% of the genes shared by its study subjects; however, all the study subjects were recruited from a single geographic area. While humans share almost all their genes, some important variations in the human genome are associated with different geographic locations, suggesting that Which choice most logically completes the text?
(C) including subjects from a wide
(A) the Human Genome Project would (B) to improve the results of the Human number of geographic areas would not
still not be completed if the number of subjects had been increased.
Genome Project subjects from many parts of the world should be included.
change the results of the Human Genome Project.
seriously flawed and must be updated (D) the Human Genome Project is to include a wider population of subjects.
(B) to improve the results of the Human Genome Project subjects from many parts of the world should be included.

A long-standing anxiety of United States steel producers is excess capacity, which means that steelmaking facilities have the ability to manufacture more steel than the market requires each year. This surplus steel would depress prices and threaten the long-term survival of the steelmaking industry. To avoid foreign steelmakers from undermining the U.S. steelmakers, tariffs have periodically been applied to imported steel. Tariffs are an extra charge that increases the price on imports. In early 2018, the U.S. government imposed new tariffs to reduce steel imports over the next two years, with the goal of stimulating domestic production. However, U.S. officials eventually concluded that those new tariffs were ineffective, noting that Which choice most effectively uses data from the graph to support the research team's conclusion?
(A) there was a reduction in steel imports from a high of 40 million metric tons in 2014 to a little above 20 million
(B) domestic steel production hovered at over 80 million metric tons per year, while exports remained consistent at
metric tons by 2019 thanks to the tariffs, approximately 10 million metric tons per
yet domestic production showed only
minor change.
year.
(C) while steel imports fell from a high of 40 million metric tons in 2014 to a little above 20 million metric tons by 2019, the reduction of exports shows that foreign countries imposed their own tariffs.
(D) steel consumption was at an all-time high in 2017 before the imposition of the tariffs, which resulted in a decline in steel consumption.

(B) domestic steel production hovered at over 80 million metric tons per year, while exports remained consistent at approximately 10 million metric tons per year, yet domestic production showed only minor change.

"A Cup of Tea" is a 1922 short story by Katherine Mansfield. In the story, Rosemary, a wealthy young woman, invites an unnamed poor woman home when she begs Rosemary for money on the street. In describing Rosemary, Mansfield contrasts the character's generosity with her self-interest, as when Mansfield writes of
Rosemary,
Which quotation from "A Cup of Tea" most effectively illustrates the claim?
(A) "Warmth, softness, light, a sweet scent, all those things so familiar to her
[Rosemary] that she never even thought about them, she watched that other receive."
(B) "Come, come upstairs,' said Rosemary, longing to begin to be generous. 'Come up to my room.' And, besides, she wanted to spare this poor little thing from being stared at by the servants; she decided as they mounted the stairs she would not even ring. but take off her things by herself."
(C) "Don't cry, poor little thing,' Rosemary said. 'Don't cry.' And she gave the other her lace handkerchief. She really was touched beyond words. She put her arm round those thin, bird- like shoulders."
(D) "Supposing she [Rosemary] took the girl home?... It would be thrilling. And she heard herself saying afterwards to the amazement of her friends: 'I simply took her home with me,' as she stepped forward and said to that dim person beside her: 'Come home to tea with me.""

(D) "Supposing she [Rosemary] took the girl home?... It would be thrilling. And she heard herself saying afterwards to the amazement of her friends: 'I simply took her home with me,' as she stepped forward and said to that dim person beside her: 'Come home to tea with me.'"

Explanation: 

This quotation most effectively illustrates the claim by highlighting Rosemary’s mixed motivations behind her act of generosity. The idea of taking the girl home is presented initially as an action that would be "thrilling" for Rosemary. This thrill is not derived from the act of helping someone in need itself, but from the anticipation of how this act would be perceived by her friends. The potential amazement of her friends seems to be a significant, if not the primary, factor in her decision. This demonstrates the contrast between her generosity (inviting the poor woman home) and her self-interest (seeking admiration and thrill from her social circle by doing so).

The consumption of meat is rapidly increasing throughout the world, so food and animal scientists are researching more efficient methods to produce the animal feed required. Since fish and chicken, two popular meat animals, eat insect larvae in their natural environments, one possible sustainable source for animal feed is the larvae of the black soldier fly (Hermetia illucens). In the wild, Hermetia illucens feeds on rotting fruit, preferring a diet rich in carbohydrates and low in protein. Niels Eriksen claims that brewery waste, despite being low in carbohydrates and high in protein, could be recycled as food for Hermetia illucens. Which finding from Eriksen's research, if true, would most directly support his claim?
(A) The Hermetia illucens larvae grew more quickly on a diet high in carbohydrates and low in protein than when provided a diet low in carbohydrates and high in protein.
(B) When the Hermetia illucens larvae raised on brewery waste were analyzed, they were found to contain two nutrients that are theorized to improve bone mass in animals.
(C) When compared to larvae raised on
rotting fruit, the Hermetia illucens larvae raised on brewery waste grew at the same rate and contained similar levels of nutrients.
(D) Both the Hermetia illucens larvae fed brewery waste and those fed rotting fruit were also observed to eat carrion, which is high in protein and low in carbohydrates.
(C) When compared to larvae raised on rotting fruit, the Hermetia illucens larvae raised on brewery waste grew at the same rate and contained similar levels of nutrients.
explantation

Niels Eriksen's claim revolves around the viability of using brewery waste as an alternative food source for Hermetia illucens larvae, despite its high protein and low carbohydrate content, which is opposite to their natural preference for a diet rich in carbohydrates and low in protein. The most direct support for this claim would be evidence demonstrating that the larvae can not only survive but also thrive on this unconventional diet in a manner comparable to their growth on their natural diet.

Option (C) provides such evidence by stating that the larvae raised on brewery waste grew at the same rate and contained similar levels of nutrients as those raised on their preferred diet of rotting fruit. This indicates that the larvae are able to adapt to a diet that significantly diverges from their natural preference, without any negative impact on their growth rate or nutritional content. This finding directly supports Eriksen's claim because it demonstrates that brewery waste can indeed serve as a sustainable, alternative source of feed for the larvae, despite its deviation from the natural dietary composition preferred by Hermetia illucens.

"On the Death of the Rev. Mr. George Whitefield" is a 1770 poem written by Phillis Wheatley. The poem praises Whitefield's efforts to build the nation by guiding young Which quotation from "On the Death of the Rev. Mr. George Whitefield" most effectively illustrates the claim?
people:
(A) "Thou didst in strains of eloquence refined / Inflame the heart, and captivate the mind."
(B) "New England deeply feels, the orphans mourn, / Their more than father will no more return."
(C) "We hear no more the music of thy tongue, / Thy wonted auditories cease to throng./Thy sermons in unequalled accents flowed, / And every bosom with devotion glowed;"
(D) "He longed to see America excel; / He charged its youth that every grace divine / Should with full lustre in their conduct shine;"

(D) "He longed to see America excel; / He charged its youth that every grace divine / Should with full lustre in their conduct shine;"

Explanation: This quotation from Phillis Wheatley's poem explicitly addresses George Whitefield's efforts towards the betterment and guidance of young people, as highlighted in the claim. It illustrates Whitefield's desire for America's progress ("He longed to see America excel") and his specific exhortation to the youth to embody and exhibit virtuous qualities ("He charged its youth that every grace divine / Should with full lustre in their conduct shine;"). This directly illustrates the idea that Whitefield played a significant role in building the nation by nurturing its young population, making it the most effective illustration of the claim among the given options.

Called both the founder and builder of Tibetan cinema, Pema Tseden was the first filmmaker to authentically represent Tibetan life. In addition to filming exclusively in Tibet, mostly around Guide, where he was raised, Tseden used only the Tibetan language and primarily employed Tibetan actors in his films. In a paper for a film class, a student claims that Tseden's Tibetan influence extends to his cinematography-that the way Tseden used a film camera also reflected his immersion in Tibetan life. Which finding, if true, would most directly support the student's claim?
(A) Tseden's films are known for using "the long take"--where the camera remains on a single scene for over ten minutes-because Tseden explains that the "Tibetan way of looking" is to gaze steadily for an extended period of time.
(B) Tseden encouraged many of his friends to study film because when he started making movies there were no cinematographers, sound recorders, set designers, or other industry professionals in Tibet.
(C) Tseden also wrote novels and short stories, and his screenwriting showed this influence, telling simple stories of everyday life.
(D) Tseden became enamored with films as a child, watching Chinese movies in his mountain village, and studied at the Beijing Film Academy.

(A) Tseden's films are known for using "the long take"--where the camera remains on a single scene for over ten minutes-because Tseden explains that the "Tibetan way of looking" is to gaze steadily for an extended period of time.

Explanation: This finding directly links Tseden's cinematographic technique to a cultural trait or perspective, i.e., the "Tibetan way of looking." It suggests that Tseden intentionally incorporates aspects of Tibetan culture into his filmmaking process, specifically his use of the camera. This approach to cinematography - the use of the long take - is explicitly justified by Tseden as reflective of a Tibetan practice or way of viewing the world. Thus, this evidence strongly supports the student's claim that Tseden's Tibetan influence extends to his cinematography, indicating that his methods of filming are a deliberate reflection of his immersion in Tibetan life.
